figures.caption: Figure 5: The CT-GMARL Spatial-Temporal Cell. Local SIEM observations are spatially fused across the dynamically masked network topology via Graph Attention (GAT). Between observations, the hidden state continuously drifts via a Neural ODE, scaled by the exact time jump ∆tk.

Figure 4: The Sim2Real Bridge Architecture. Models are trained rapidly in the highly-parallelized MockHypervisor. During validation, the environment is toggled to DockerHypervisor, routing commands (e.g., ExploitEternalBlue) to execute genuine Python payload scripts against live targets.

Figure 1: Architectural comparison of environmental time-modeling. (a) Standard RL wargames force synchronous execution. (b) NetForge_RL utilizes a POSMDP framework where actions have realistic durations, and NLP telemetry (Oi) arrives asynchronously. Crucially, the Green Agent generates stochastic background noise (e.g., event O5), producing false-positive alerts that punctuate the continuous integration and force the defender to distinguish adversarial signals from background noise.
Figure 10: Aggregate Reward Yields. The CT-GMARL learning curve (pink) converges to a median reward of 57,135, achieving a 2.0× improvement over R-MAPPO (orange, 28,347).

Figure 3: NetForge_RL Topological constraints. The environment physically restricts traversal to the Secure Vault. Agents cannot simply rely on graph proximity; they must execute complex, multi-step Identity and Access Management (IAM) exploits to acquire ZTNA tokens prior to lateral movement.
